name: Changing Direction

location.name: Goethe-Institut / Max Mueller Bhavan New  New

description: Friday, 6 March 2026, 17:30Get your tickets here!In continuation of the series of events held to mark and celebrate 10 years since the publication was launched,The Wirewill be hosting theChanging Direction Short Film FestivalatGoethe-Institut/Max Mueller Bhavan, the venue partner. The festival is sponsored by theInformation and Public Relations Department, Government of Kerala.The call for films had a broad theme: Changing Direction. Filmmakers were free to interpret this in any way they liked, and submit films of 20 minutes or less. The jury ofPayal Kapadia,Vani SubramanianandFaiza Ahmad Khanhas chosen nine films from the nearly 200 submissions received.The final films are (in no particular order):1. Noises from the Basement, directed by Abhay P.Who makes a city, and how often do we see them? This film provides a view of Ahmedabad and its growth – but seen from the Basement.2. Daddu Zindabad, directed by Samar JainIn a village steeped in caste discrimination, Vishnu must defy oppressive traditions to fulfil his grandfather’s dying wish – to be cremated in the village’s only cremation ground controlled by the ‘upper’ castes.3. The Waiting Game, directed by Kanika GuptaThe Waiting Gamefollows the arrest of Indian fishermen in the Arabian Sea and how it upends the lives of the women they leave behind.4. Ek Achha Sawaal, directed by Sara SainiIn Punjab’s Banga district, sculptor Davinder crafts ‘showpiece’ water tankers, serving customers whose striking designs reflect their migration stories, shaped by caste, class, unemployment, and the declining agricultural economy.5. WE, the Puffcorns of India, directed by Aditya MungdeOn their journey to a new life in Pune, Shilpi, a determined woman migrating for work, faces a heart-wrenching decision when her drunken husband gets separated from them during a celebratory Ram Mandir consecration rally.6. KYC*, directed by Aravindan S.S.By day, Ganesh Rajasekharan navigates his workplace with patronising authority, dismissing female colleagues and clients while championing traditional masculinity. By night, G posts misogynistic and religious vitriol on social media, targeting self assured women with hate.7. Avaar Navaar Chhoriyaan, directed by Shefalee Jain and Shivi Bhatnagarअवार-नवार छोरियां /Every Now and Then Girlsis an adaptation of a Rajasthani folktale,‘Dohri Joon’/Two Lives, collected by the renowned folklorist of Rajasthan, Vijaydan Detha.8. Tara, directed by Ashutosh S. ShankarTarais a story of an Indian Dalit trans woman who puts herself out in the dating pool of Bombay as she tries to navigate her queer and caste identity.9. Kal Raat Kolaba Mein, directed by Suryansh Deo SrivastavaWhen Ashfaq, a food delivery executive, is lynched by a mob, five witnesses are summoned for interrogation.The aim of this festival was to celebrate creativity and free expression, andThe Wireis thrilled to be able to share these films with you and to provide a space where the filmmakers can interact with the audience.Congratulating all the filmmakers who participated and those who were selected, jury head Kapadia said, “It was really incredible to see the range of work, as well as the political passion in each of them. The chosen films have a very clear point of view, as well as well as an artistic voice that is unique. It was a true pleasure to watch them.”There is no parking available at the venue.Get your tickets now!Please download save the ticket that is generated after you pay. In case you misplace that ticket, please emailevents@thewire.in.

name: Kapital und Ressentiment / Capital and Ressentiment

location.name: Goethe-Institut / Max Mueller Bhavan New  New

description: The lecture deals with the question of how the development of new forms of enterprise in digital capitalism combines with the weakening of democratic structures. The internet industry is understood as a renewal of a financial regime that has been forming since the 1970s and has tapped into a new source of value creation through the management of information. The privatization of networks has led to the emergence of new media corporations whose business consists of appropriating public infrastructures and supplying opinion markets.The resulting debates about fragmented publics, polarization, and the loss of democracy are taken as an opportunity to explore the interrelationship between economic processes and affect economies. The social affect of ressentiment occupies a privileged position in this context: in the current economic system, it functions both as a product and a productive force, contributing to the stabilization of information capitalism precisely through its political and social erosive powers.Register here© SchulthessBiographyJoseph Vogl|© Joseph VoglJoseph Vogl was Professor of Modern German Literature, Cultural and Media Studies at Humboldt-Universität zu Berlin (2006-2023) and is Regular Visiting Professor at Princeton University (since 2007). He studied modern literature, philosophy, history, and linguistics in Munich and Paris (Promotion 1990, Habilitation 2000), and has taught and researched at various reputed institutions. For his work he received several awards: Translation Prize for the Promotion of Franco-German Relations (1988), shortlist for the Leipzig Book Fair Prize 2015 in the category of non-fiction (“Der Souveränitätseffekt”, 2015), Günther-Anders-Prize forCritical Thinking (2022).He was the director or principal investigator of a range of research projects including“Media History” (Research Training Group), “Esthetic Experience” (Collaborative Research Center), “The Knowledge of Literature” (international PhD-Net), TOPOI (Cluster of Excellence), “Small Forms in the History of Literature and Sciences” (Research Training Group), and “Matters of Activity: Image Space Material” (Cluster of Excellence).Among his most important written works are the monographs “Ort der Gewalt. Kafkas literarische Ethik” (1990); “Kalkül und Leidenschaft. Poetik des ökonomischen Menschen” (2002); “Über das Zaudern” with the latest one being"Meteor. Versuch über das Schwebende" (2025). He is also well known for his intensive work on modern and present-day finance and economics, analyzing finance theories from the perspective of cultural history. This work culminated in his books“Das Gespenst des Kapitals” (2010; “The Specter of Capital”, 2014), “Der Souveränitätseffekt” (2015; “The Ascendancy of Finance”, 2017) and “Kapital und Ressentiment. Eine kurze Theorie der Gegenwart” (2021; “Capital and Ressentiment: A Brief Theory of the Present”, 2022), which received wide recognition in the fields of cultural studies as well as economics, and initiated a public debate. Further workings include translations from French to German (Deleuze, Guattari, Lévi-Strauss, Lyotard) and numerous interviews on TV with Alexander Kluge (see“Soll und Haben. Fernsehgespräche”, 2009; “Senkblei der Geschichten. Gespräche”, 2020).

name: 21st Asian Women’s Film Festival (AWFF)

location.name: India International Centre (IIC) 40 Max Mueller Marg New

keywords: Organised by IAWRT Chapter India, in collaboration with the India International Centre
description: Friday, 13–Sunday, 15 March 2026Registerherefor the film festival.The 21st edition of theAsian Women’s Film Festival (AWFF), organised by the India Chapter of theInternational Association of Women in Radio and Television (IAWRT), continues a journey that began in 2005 when Jai Chandiram of IAWRT and Kapila Vatsayan from the Asia project at the India International Centre envisioned a space where women filmmakers from across Asia could share their work, find audiences, and build community. Two decades on, these founding impulses remain at the heart of the festival, as does the collaboration between the IIC and IAWRT.AWFFstands today as one of the longest-running women’s film festivals in India and Asia, sustained by the voluntary labour, care, and commitment of IAWRT members and allies who believe in feminist media practice and collective viewing cultures.Over the years, the festival has created a vital platform for women’s cinematic voices across forms and geographies. It has enabled filmmakers to present work that traverses the personal and the political, the intimate and the structural, and to situate their stories within wider feminist solidarities across Asia. The festival also affirms the continuing importance of experiencing cinema together – in a shared space where images, sounds, and conversations unfold collectively.This year’s edition presents approximately 80 films from across Asia and beyond, spanning documentary, fiction, animation, student, and curated regional programmes. The films reflect a wide spectrum of concerns – from labour, ecology, migration, memory, and identity to interior landscapes of desire, belonging, and resistance – articulated through diverse cinematic languages.Alongside short and feature-length works, the festival includes a special focus on animation through collaborations with partner festivals and initiatives such asAniMela, foregrounding women’s creative leadership in a field historically shaped by gendered exclusions. A curated package from Central Asia (Kazakhstan and the Kyrgyz Republic) further expands the festival’s regional dialogues, bringing lesser-seen cinematic voices into conversation with South Asian contexts.AWFFhas always extended beyond screenings into processes of learning, exchange, and reflection. The 2026 programme includes workshops, masterclasses, and discussions that address both practice and pedagogy. A half-day introductory filmmaking workshop invites young and participants into the basics of cinematic storytelling. A round-table on film pedagogy and women’s inclusion brings together scholars and educators to reflect on gendered gaps within film education; the outcomes will be shared with film schools to encourage more equitable frameworks. Renowned Japanese film editorKeiko Okawawill conduct a masterclass, sharing insights from her long and influential engagement with cinematic form and editing practice. Well known filmmaker,Paromita Vohrawill take another masterclass.The festival also continues its commitment to intergenerational dialogue – between established practitioners, emerging filmmakers, students, and audiences. Each edition ofAWFFbecomes a temporary community: a space where professional and personal worlds meet, friendships are renewed, collaborations are imagined, and new viewers encounter the breadth of women’s cinematic expression.As the festival enters its third decade, it carries forward a simple yet powerful belief: that women’s stories, images, and creative labour deserve visibility, dialogue, and celebration – and that feminist film cultures are sustained through shared spaces of watching, thinking, and being together.We look forward to welcoming audiences, students, filmmakers, and friends from across Delhi and beyond to three days of cinema and conversation at the21st Asian Women’s Film Festival.
